children.name: Bombycilla
children.description: Plump crested silky-plumaged birds named for the red wax-like tips on their secondary feathers. They breed in boreal Sweden's spruce-birch forest, subsisting on berries and insects. In winter they irrupt southward when the northern rowan crop fails — a waxwing invasion stripping Stockholm's ornamental rowans is one of the great Scandinavian winter spectacles.

children.name: Bombycilla garrulus
children.description: A plump, crested, silky bird with waxy red wingtips that breeds in the boreal forest and irrupts south in winter when the rowan-berry crop fails. Flocks descending on Stockholm's ornamental rowans are a classic Swedish winter spectacle.

children.name: Bombycilla cedrorum
children.description: The most widespread and abundant waxwing, resident across much of North America and wintering south to Panama. Named for its fondness for cedar berries, it also gorges on crabapples, serviceberries, and other fruits — sometimes consuming so many fermented berries that flocks become visibly intoxicated. Distinguished from the Bohemian Waxwing by its yellow-washed belly, white undertail coverts, and absence of yellow and white wing markings.

children.name: Bombycilla japonica
children.description: The rarest and least-known waxwing, breeding in the taiga forests of the Russian Far East and wintering in Japan, Korea, and northeastern China. It is the only waxwing that lacks the red waxy secondary tips — instead it sports a striking crimson tail band. Near Threatened due to deforestation on its wintering grounds, it is a sought-after rarity for birdwatchers wherever it appears.
